name: Connecticut
regulatoryOwner: Connecticut Department of Energy and Environmental Protection
delegatedLocalAuthorities: DEEP sets the baseline, but town files, seller paperwork, and site clues often decide whether you need records, a sweep, or spill follow-up.
buyerSellerSummary: In Connecticut, buried-tank questions during a sale usually turn on paperwork first: seller disclosure, closure proof, and signs of past oil heat.
recordLookupSummary: Start with the seller disclosure, any closure permit or invoice, and oil-to-gas paperwork before you treat silence as proof.
sweepFirstSummary: A sweep makes sense when the house shows old oil-heat clues but the paperwork still does not prove whether a tank remains.
removalVsAbandonmentSummary: Closure choices depend on whether a tank is actually there, how it can be reached, and whether DEEP guidance points toward something more than routine closure.
leakReportingSummary: If odor, stained soil, or spill language shows up, the question stops being just about disclosure and starts becoming a cleanup issue.
insuranceCleanupSummary: Insurance or cleanup decisions only become useful after you know whether this is paperwork, confirmation, or release response.
quickAnswer: In Connecticut, start with the seller disclosure, closure paperwork, and site clues before you decide whether this is a sweep, tank closure, or spill problem.
commonTriggers: A buyer under contract learns the home once used heating oil.
The seller cannot produce a closure record or tank invoice.
Inspection notes mention old oil-heat clues, but no one can say whether a tank is still there.
cautions: Do not treat old oil heat as proof that a tank still exists.
Do not jump from missing paperwork to contamination assumptions.
Do not skip the seller disclosure and permit trail just because the deal is moving fast.
strongestTriggerStates: The sale is live and the parties still cannot tell whether this is missing paperwork, a remaining tank, or a spill issue.
The house has old oil-heat clues, but the seller has no clear closure proof.
Quotes are getting discussed before the paperwork and site facts line up.
firstMoves: Ask for the seller disclosure, any closure permit or contractor invoice, and oil-to-gas paperwork in one request.
Use a sweep when the site clues are stronger than the paperwork.
If anyone mentions odor, stained soil, or spill paperwork, stop treating the issue like ordinary sale-side paperwork.
documentTargets: Seller disclosure form, any DEEP-related closure paperwork, and contractor invoices tied to the tank or furnace changeover.
Oil-to-gas conversion records, burner replacement invoices, or permit history showing when oil heat ended.
Any spill, cleanup, or no-further-action document showing the property already moved beyond routine closure.
escalationSignals: Odor, sheen, stained soil, or excavation notes suggesting more than a paperwork gap.
Any file that references spill response, cleanup, or environmental review.
Facts confirming the tank is still in place and shifting the question from suspicion to closure planning.

sourceStack.title: Connecticut DEEP Residential Home Heating Oil Tanks FAQ
